title: Ribbon diagram

infobox.value: Cylindrical spiral ribbons, with ribbon plane approximately following plane of peptides.

Arrows with thickness, about one-quarter as thick as they are wide, showing direction and twist of the strand from amino to carboxy end. β-sheets are seen as unified because neighboring strands twist in unison.
Round ropes that are fatter in the foreground and thinner towards the back, following smoothed path of Cα trace.
Round rope that gradually flattens out into a thin helical ribbon.

infobox.key: Polypeptide direction, NH2 and COOH termini
infobox.value: Small arrows on one or both of the termini, or letters. For β-strands, the direction of the arrow is sufficient. Today, the direction of the polypeptide chain is often indicated by a colour ramp.
Interlocked SS symbol or a zigzag, like a stylized lightning stroke.
Shading or colour adds dimensionality to the diagram. Generally, the features at the front are the highest in contrast and those towards the back are the lowest.

Smith, Thomas J. (October 27, 2005). ["Displaying and Analyzing Atomic Structures on the Macintosh"](https://web.archive.org/web/20020328153147/http://www.danforthcenter.org/smith/molview/over/overview.html). Danforth Plant Science Center. Archived from [the original](http://www.danforthcenter.org/smith/molview/over/overview.html) on 28 March 2002.

Richardson, D. C.; Richardson, J. S. (January 2002). ["Teaching Molecular 3-D Literacy"](https://doi.org/10.1002%2Fbmb.2002.494030010005). *Biochemistry and Molecular Biology Education*. **30** (1): 21–26. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1002/bmb.2002.494030010005](https://doi.org/10.1002%2Fbmb.2002.494030010005).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

3D schematic representation of protein structure

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Myoglobin.png/250px-Myoglobin.png)](/wiki/File:Myoglobin.png)

Ribbon diagram of [myoglobin](/wiki/Myoglobin "Myoglobin") bound to [haem](/wiki/Heme "Heme") (sticks) and [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen") (red spheres) ([PDB](/wiki/Protein_Data_Bank "Protein Data Bank"): [1MBO](https://www.rcsb.org/structure/1MBO)​)

**Ribbon diagrams**, also known as **Richardson diagrams**, are [3D](/wiki/Three-dimensional_space "Three-dimensional space") schematic representations of [protein structure](/wiki/Protein_structure "Protein structure") and are one of the most common methods of protein depiction used today. The ribbon depicts the general course and organization of the protein backbone in 3D and serves as a visual framework for hanging details of the entire atomic structure, such as the balls for the oxygen atoms attached to myoglobin's active site in the adjacent figure. Ribbon diagrams are generated by interpolating a smooth curve through the [polypeptide](/wiki/Polypeptide "Polypeptide") backbone. [α-helices](/wiki/Alpha_helix "Alpha helix") are shown as coiled ribbons or thick tubes, [β-sheets](/wiki/Beta_sheet "Beta sheet") as arrows, and non-repetitive coils or loops as lines or thin tubes. The direction of the [polypeptide chain](/wiki/Peptide "Peptide") is shown locally by the arrows, and may be indicated overall by a colour ramp along the length of the ribbon.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Ribbon diagrams are simple yet powerful, expressing the visual basics of a molecular structure (twist, fold and unfold). This method has successfully portrayed the overall organization of protein structures, reflecting their three-dimensional nature and allowing better understanding of these complex objects both by expert structural biologists and by other scientists, students,[[2]](#cite_note-2) and the general public.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/TriosePhosphateIsomerase_Ribbon_pastel_photo_mat.png/250px-TriosePhosphateIsomerase_Ribbon_pastel_photo_mat.png)](/wiki/File:TriosePhosphateIsomerase_Ribbon_pastel_photo_mat.png)

Ribbon schematic of [triose P isomerase](/wiki/Triosephosphate_isomerase "Triosephosphate isomerase") monomer (hand-drawn by [J. Richardson](/wiki/Jane_S._Richardson "Jane S. Richardson"), 1981) ([PDB](/wiki/Protein_Data_Bank "Protein Data Bank"): [1TIM](https://www.rcsb.org/structure/1TIM)​)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ribbon_diagram&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The first ribbon diagrams, hand-drawn by [Jane S. Richardson](/wiki/Jane_S._Richardson "Jane S. Richardson") in 1980 (influenced by earlier individual illustrations),[[3]](#cite_note-earlyRib-3) were the first schematics of 3D protein structure to be produced systematically.[[3]](#cite_note-earlyRib-3)[[4]](#cite_note-MethEnz-4) They were created to illustrate a classification of protein structures for an article in *Advances in Protein Chemistry*[[5]](#cite_note-Adv-5) (now available in annotated form on-line at [Anatax](http://kinemage.biochem.duke.edu/teaching/anatax/) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20190316165752/http://kinemage.biochem.duke.edu/teaching/anatax/) 2019-03-16 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine")). These drawings were outlined in pen on tracing paper over a printout of a [Cα](/wiki/Alpha_and_beta_carbon "Alpha and beta carbon") trace of the atomic coordinates, and shaded with colored pencil or pastels;[[6]](#cite_note-6) they preserved positions, smoothed the backbone path, and incorporated small local shifts to disambiguate the visual appearance.[[4]](#cite_note-MethEnz-4) As well as the triose isomerase ribbon drawing at the right, other hand-drawn examples depicted [prealbumin](https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?title=File:PrealbuminDimer_ribbon.jpg), [flavodoxin](https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?title=File:Flavodoxin_ribbon_BWline.jpg), and [Cu,Zn superoxide dismutase](https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?title=File:2SOD_ribbon_pastel.jpg).

In 1982, [Arthur M. Lesk](/wiki/Arthur_M._Lesk "Arthur M. Lesk") and co-workers first enabled the automatic generation of ribbon diagrams through a computational implementation that uses [Protein Data Bank](/wiki/Protein_Data_Bank "Protein Data Bank") files as input.[[7]](#cite_note-7) This conceptually simple [algorithm](/wiki/Algorithm "Algorithm") fit [cubic polynomial](/wiki/Cubic_polynomial "Cubic polynomial") [B-spline](/wiki/B-spline "B-spline") curves to the peptide planes. Most modern graphics systems provide either B-splines or [Hermite splines](/wiki/Hermite_spline "Hermite spline") as a basic drawing primitive. One type of spline implementation passes through each Cα guide point, producing an exact but choppy curve. Both hand-drawn and most computer ribbons (such as those shown here) are smoothed over about four successive guide points (usually the peptide midpoint) to produce a more visually pleasing and understandable representation. To give the right radius for helical spirals while preserving smooth β-strands, the splines can be modified by offsets proportional to local curvature, as first developed by Mike Carson for his Ribbons program[[8]](#cite_note-8) and later adopted by other molecular graphics software, such as the open-source Mage program for [kinemage](/wiki/Kinemage "Kinemage") graphics[[9]](#cite_note-9) that produced the ribbon image at top right (other examples: [1XK8 trimer](https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?title=File:1xk8_trimer_ribbon.jpg) and [DNA polymerase](https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?title=File:DNApolymerase-DNA_complex_2HHV_ribbon.jpg)).

Since their inception, and continuing in the present, ribbon diagrams have been the single most common representation of protein structure and a common choice of cover image for a journal or textbook.

### Current computer programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ribbon_diagram&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Current computer programs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Tubby-1c8z-pymol.png/250px-Tubby-1c8z-pymol.png)](/wiki/File:Tubby-1c8z-pymol.png)

[PyMol](/wiki/PyMol "PyMol") ribbon of the structure of the [tubby](/wiki/Tubby_protein "Tubby protein") protein ([PDB](/wiki/Protein_Data_Bank "Protein Data Bank"): [1C8Z](https://www.rcsb.org/structure/1C8Z)​)

One popular program used for drawing ribbon diagrams is [Molscript](/w/index.php?title=Molscript&action=edit&redlink=1 "Molscript (page does not exist)"). Molscript utilizes [Hermite splines](/wiki/Hermite_spline "Hermite spline") to create coordinates for coils, turns, strands, and helices. The curve passes through all its control points ([Cα](/wiki/C%CE%B1 "Cα") atoms) guided by direction vectors. The program was built based on traditional molecular graphics by [Arthur M. Lesk](/wiki/Arthur_M._Lesk "Arthur M. Lesk"), Karl Hardman, and John Priestle.[[10]](#cite_note-10) [Jmol](/wiki/Jmol "Jmol") is an open-source Java-based viewer for browsing molecular structures on the web; it includes a simplified "cartoon" version of ribbons. Other graphics programs such as DeepView (example: [urease](https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?title=File:Urease-1E9Z.jpg)) and MolMol (example: [SH2 domain](https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?title=File:1lkkA_SH2_domain.png)) also produce ribbon images. KiNG[[11]](#cite_note-11) is the Java-based successor to Mage (examples: [α-hemolysin top view](https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?title=File:AlphaHemolysin_membrane_heptamer_7AHL_VwDownPore.jpg) and [side view](https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?title=File:AlphaHemolysin_membrane_heptamer_7AHL_sideVw.jpg)).

[UCSF Chimera](/wiki/UCSF_Chimera "UCSF Chimera") is a powerful molecular modeling program that also includes [visualizations](/wiki/Scientific_visualization "Scientific visualization") such as ribbons, notable especially for the ability to combine them with contoured shapes from cryo-[electron microscopy](/wiki/Electron_microscopy "Electron microscopy") data.[[12]](#cite_note-12) [PyMOL](/wiki/PyMOL "PyMOL"), by [Warren DeLano](/wiki/Warren_DeLano "Warren DeLano"),[[13]](#cite_note-13) is a popular and flexible molecular graphics program (based on [Python](/wiki/Python_(programming_language) "Python (programming language)")) that operates in interactive mode and also produces presentation-quality 2D images for ribbon diagrams and many other representations.

## Features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ribbon_diagram&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Features")]

[![Spiral helix ribbons, beta-strand arrows, and smoothed loops, hand-drawn by Jane Richardson](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Ribbon-drawing_3-techniques_v.jpg/250px-Ribbon-drawing_3-techniques_v.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ribbon-drawing_3-techniques_v.jpg)

| Secondary structure[[4]](#cite_note-MethEnz-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Adv-5) |  |
| --- | --- |
| α-Helices | Cylindrical spiral ribbons, with ribbon plane approximately following plane of peptides. |
| β-Strands | Arrows with thickness, about one-quarter as thick as they are wide, showing direction and twist of the strand from amino to carboxy end. β-sheets are seen as unified because neighboring strands twist in unison. |
| Loops and miscellaneous |  |
| Nonrepetitive loops | Round ropes that are fatter in the foreground and thinner towards the back, following smoothed path of Cα trace. |
| Junctions between loops and helices | Round rope that gradually flattens out into a thin helical ribbon. |
| Other features |  |
| Polypeptide direction, NH2 and COOH termini | Small arrows on one or both of the termini, or letters. For β-strands, the direction of the arrow is sufficient. Today, the direction of the polypeptide chain is often indicated by a colour ramp. |
| Disulfide bonds | Interlocked SS symbol or a zigzag, like a stylized lightning stroke. |
| Prosthetic groups or inhibitors | Stick figures, or [ball & stick](/wiki/Ball-and-stick_model "Ball-and-stick model"). |
| Metals | Spheres. |
| Shading and colour | Shading or colour adds dimensionality to the diagram. Generally, the features at the front are the highest in contrast and those towards the back are the lowest. |

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ribbon_diagram&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Molecular graphics](/wiki/Molecular_graphics "Molecular graphics")
